short.headline: ₹357 lakh crore, $3.9 trillion, 5th in the world, and still ₹2.5 lakh per person
short.dek: India’s economy is both enormous and poor. The total output places it among the top five, but the average income is lower than many Asian peers.
short.body: Add up everything India produced in 2025-26 and you get about ₹357 lakh crore, around $3.9 trillion at the market exchange rate. That makes it roughly the world’s fifth-largest economy. Yet divide that sum among 1.4 billion people and the average is only about ₹2.5 lakh a year, or $2,700. The same India that is a global economic heavyweight is, person by person, still a low-income country. This page explains that tension through the head­line size, what is real and what is inflation, how India compares with others, what it produces, who spends, who saves, and what the GDP number hides.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: Think of India as a massive chai stall that serves 1.4 billion people in a single day. The total bill is enormous — that’s our GDP. But when you split the bill equally, each person’s share is tiny. That’s GDP per capita. Most of the massive number you hear in headlines is just things getting more expensive over the years, not actually more cups of chai being poured. And because our population is so large, even being the fifth-biggest stall in the world doesn’t make the average person rich. The real story is in what India makes — we skipped becoming a factory like China and jumped straight to services — and in who is doing the spending: ordinary people like you and me, consuming, saving in banks and gold, and slowly building the country’s wealth.
macha.soWhat: India is a giant with a long way to go — the total economy is huge, but the average person’s share is still very small.

article.title: How big is India’s economy?
article.standfirst: The same country that is the world’s fifth-largest economy has a per person output of barely ₹2.5 lakh a year. This is the most complete single-page guide to India’s macroeconomy, built directly on the official National Accounts.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How big is India’s economy in rupees?

In 2025-26, India’s gross domestic product, the total market value of all final goods and services produced inside the country, touched about **₹357.1 lakh crore**. That is the figure the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MOSPI) publishes as the First Advance Estimate, and it will be revised. The climb from the early years of the Republic is astonishing. In 1950-51, India’s economy was only about **₹10,221.6 crore**. A new television, a village haircut, a software engineer’s code, a share-auto ride, every final transaction contributes to this number, and the sum has multiplied roughly 3,500 times in seventy-five years. But that multiplication is not all new activity. A large part of it is the rupee losing value over time, which the next chart untangles.

## How much of that number is just prices?

The ₹357 lakh crore figure is nominal GDP: output valued at whatever prices prevailed last year. When you strip out inflation by revaluing everything at constant 2011-12 prices, the economy shrinks to about **₹201.9 lakh crore**. For comparison, in 1950-51 real GDP was only about **₹5 lakh crore**. The widening gap between the two lines is accumulated inflation over decades. When economists say the economy ‘grew about 7%’ they are measuring the real line, not the nominal one. If a dosa cost ₹10 ten years ago and ₹25 today, and you ate the same number of dosas, your real consumption is unchanged even though nominal spending rose. This is why the real series is the one that matters for understanding actual expansion.

## What does that mean per person?

Divide the national cake into 1.4 billion slices and each slice comes to about **₹2.5 lakh** a year in 2025-26. Back in the early 1960s, the earliest year for this series, it was only **₹404** per person. This is an arithmetic average: GDP divided by the mid-year population. It is not a salary, not a typical income, and certainly not what a farmer in Mandsaur or a salesman in Madurai actually earns. A small number of high earners pull the average up, which is why the real picture of inequality needs a separate page. But the trend is clear: the average Indian’s measured output has risen enormously even after adjusting for prices, from about ₹16,905 in 1961 at constant 2011-12 prices to about ₹1.4 lakh now. Yet most people feel far from rich. The next comparison explains why.

## How does India compare globally?

India’s GDP measured at the market exchange rate, converting rupees at the going dollar price, was about **$3.92 trillion** in 2025, according to the IMF. That places it roughly fifth among all countries. But when the IMF revalues the same output at purchasing-power parity, which adjusts for the fact that a rupee buys much more in India than a dollar buys in the United States, the figure jumps to about **$17.3 trillion**, the third-largest in the world behind only China and the United States. PPP is larger because the average price level in India is lower: a haircut, a plate of biryani, or a month’s rent costs far fewer dollars here, so the same physical output translates into a higher PPP estimate. Both numbers are correct; they answer different questions. Market rates tell you the financial weight of the economy in global trade and investment; PPP tells you the volume of goods and services being produced relative to other countries.

## Where does India stand among the big economies?

At market exchange rates, India at **$3.92 trillion** is neck and neck with the United Kingdom at **$4.00 trillion**, while Japan stands at **$4.44 trillion** and Germany at **$5.05 trillion**. Only the United States and China, with economies more than four times the size, are far ahead. India overtook the UK around 2022, but because these numbers are in dollars, the ranking can swap from year to year as the rupee and pound move. The closeness means the 5th slot is not fixed; it is a moving photo, not a permanent position. What matters more than the precise slot is the distance that remains: India’s total economy is just over one-fifth the size of China’s and a little under one-eighth the size of America’s.

## Why is India still poor per person?

Divide those huge totals by population and the ranking inverts. India’s per capita GDP in 2025 was about **$2,675**. That is barely above Bangladesh, whose figure stood at about **$2,636**. Indonesia (about **$5,082**) and Vietnam (about **$4,829**) are already twice as high. China, at about **$13,968**, is five times higher, and South Korea, at about **$36,227**, is more than twelve times higher. India is large as a country and poor as a people because a large output divided by a very large population yields a small average. Per person output is the starting point for thinking about living standards, but it still hides distribution, a Keralite and a Bihari do not get similar slices.

## What does India actually produce?

The composition of output has changed profoundly since independence. Agriculture’s share of gross value added has fallen from **53.2%** in 1950-51 to **16.8%** today. Services have climbed from **30.6%** to **56.4%**, making it the dominant sector. Industry sits at **26.8%**, having risen modestly from 16.2%. This is the classic story of a developing economy: farms giving way to offices, call centres, IT firms, delivery apps. GVA is the producers’ view, it measures the value each sector adds before adding product taxes and subtracting subsidies. GDP is GVA plus those net taxes, which is why the sector charts are labelled GVA and the headline is GDP.

## Why didn’t India become a factory nation?

The real surprise is manufacturing. Its share of GVA has barely budged: from about **12%** in 1951 to **14.1%** today. Unlike China, South Korea or Japan, which became factory‑led economies, India skipped mass manufacturing. It transitioned directly from farms to services, a pattern that leaves a gap in formal, steady jobs for millions of less‑skilled workers. The shops and phone assembly lines have grown, but not enough to pull in the large number of people still dependent on low‑productivity farm work. ‘Make in India’ is the attempt to change this, and the flat line in the chart is the most visible reason why.

## Who is doing the spending?

The same GDP can be read from the spending side: who buys the output. In 2025‑26, private consumption accounted for **61.5%** of GDP. Gross fixed capital formation, investment in factories, roads, machines, took **30%**. Government consumption was **9.9%**, and net exports were a small drag at **-2.3%** because imports (roughly 24% of GDP) slightly exceed exports (about 21%). India is a consumption‑led economy. The investment share is the number economists watch most: it is the seed of future growth, and at 30% it is respectable but lower than China’s heights during its fastest decades.

## How did trade reshape the economy?

In 1950‑51, exports amounted to just **7.2%** of GDP and imports to **7%**. Through the decades of licence‑raj, India was a near‑closed economy. After the 1991 crisis and the liberalisation that followed, both flows surged. By 2025‑26, exports had risen to **21.5%** of GDP and imports to **23.7%**. Even though net exports remain negative, the economy is vastly more integrated with the world. A farmer now sells soyabean to a global market, a software engineer works for a client in California, and in return the country buys crude oil and electronic components. The openness has lifted many but also makes India more sensitive to global shocks.

## Does the output belong to Indians?

GDP measures everything produced inside India’s borders. But some of that output is owned by foreigners, through profits, dividends and interest that leave the country. Gross National Income, which counts income accruing to Indians, stood at **₹351.6 lakh crore** in 2025‑26, about **₹5.5 lakh crore** less than GDP. That gap has grown over time as foreign investment in India increased. Yet one step further, Gross National Disposable Income, GNI plus remittances sent home by Indians working abroad, rises to about **₹362 lakh crore**, higher than GDP. The remittances of nurses, drivers and software professionals in the Gulf and elsewhere more than offset the income paid to foreigners. For a country as big on migration as India, the largest income concept is not GDP but GNDI.

## Who saves the money that India invests?

India saves about **30.7%** of its GDP, one of the highest rates anywhere, and that saving finances domestic investment. The big saver is not the government or big companies; it is households. In 2023‑24, households directly saved about **18.1%** of GDP. Private‑corporate saving added **10.7%**, and the public sector (government and state enterprises) saved a tiny **2%**. Households park their savings in bank deposits, gold, provident funds, insurance and small property, the familiar instruments of a khata‑keeping family. Without that steady flow of household thrift, the country’s roads, ports and factory sheds could not be built.

## How fast is India’s economy growing?

Real GDP growth, which removes inflation, is usually in the 5 to 8% range. In 2025‑26, the estimate is **7.4%**. The earliest reading in this series, 2012‑13, was **5.5%**. The line is jagged: it can dip sharply when a drought hits or a global crisis erupts. It actually shrank during the COVID lockdown year, a rare outright contraction, before rebounding strongly. India remains among the fastest‑growing large economies, but a single bad monsoon or a financial shock can knock a whole percentage point off the annual figure, so a single year’s number never tells the whole story.

## What does GDP still not tell us?

GDP is a brilliant measure of the size and growth of production. It is a poor measure of wellbeing. It does not show who gets the income, the same average can sit above stark inequality. It misses most unpaid work: the cooking, cleaning, and care that keep families and the economy running. It does not subtract the cost of polluted air or depleted groundwater. It says nothing about whether jobs are secure, formal, or fairly paid; a large chunk of India’s economy is informal and imperfectly measured. And it ignores the huge gap between, say, Maharashtra and Bihar. Use GDP to know how big the pie is and how fast it is baking; look elsewhere to know whether everyone gets a fair slice.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: India’s headline GDP is about ₹357 lakh crore, the world’s 5th largest by market exchange rates and 3rd by purchasing power. But per person it is only ₹2.5 lakh a year, an average that hides deep inequality and a structural transformation from farms to services that largely skipped manufacturing.

pullQuotes.quote: India’s per person output is about ₹2.5 lakh, but that’s an average, not a salary.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of the entire country as one big kitchen. GDP is the total value of all dishes cooked inside that kitchen in a year. It includes only the final dishes — a biryani served to a customer, not the rice and spices used to make it. It does not matter whether the cook is Indian or foreign; if the dish is made inside India’s borders, it counts. GDP tells you how much production happened, not how well the citizens are doing.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This is the central measure of the page; every other concept builds on it.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: If you divide the total dishes cooked in the kitchen equally among everyone in the country, you get GDP per capita. It is like saying each Indian gets about ₹2.5 lakh worth of goods and services produced in the country in a year. But in reality, some get much more and most get far less because the slices are not equal. It is an average, not a typical income.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Used to show the big-but-poor story and to compare living standards across countries.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Imagine you buy the same vegetables every week but their prices keep rising. The nominal cost goes up, but your real consumption — how many kilos you actually eat — might be unchanged. Nominal GDP values output at today’s prices; real GDP uses prices from a fixed base year (2011-12 in India’s case) to remove the effect of inflation. Real GDP tells you if the country genuinely produced more, or just paid more.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Explains why the ₹357 lakh crore figure is mostly price rises, not just extra production.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A rupee buys different things in different places. A haircut that costs ₹300 in India might cost $30 in the US. At the market exchange rate, ₹300 is roughly $3.6, making the Indian haircut look much cheaper. PPP adjusts for these price differences so that one dollar buys the same basket of goods everywhere. By PPP, India looks larger because its lower prices mean its output can buy more.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Shifts India’s global ranking and makes per capita comparisons fairer by accounting for cost of living.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Every producer — a farmer, a factory, a salon — adds some value to the raw materials they start with. GVA sums up that value added by each sector. It is the production view of the economy. GDP is GVA plus net taxes (like GST) minus subsidies. So when you see a chart of sector shares, it is GVA, because it shows the contributors directly.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: All sector share charts on this page use GVA, so understanding the term prevents confusion.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: GDP is ‘what is produced inside the country.’ GNI is ‘what income belongs to Indians.’ If an American company makes a profit in India and sends it home, that profit is in India’s GDP but not in India’s GNI. If an Indian nurse in Dubai sends money home, that income is in India’s GNI but not in India’s GDP. The difference is net primary income from abroad.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Shows that not all output stays with Indians, and remittances bring money back.

chartExplainers.title: India's economy, 1950-51 to today
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s nominal GDP has risen from about ₹10,000 crore at Independence to ₹357.1 lakh crore in 2025-26.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the total market value of all final goods and services produced inside India each year, in current rupees. The vertical axis is in lakh crore rupees; the horizontal runs from 1950-51 to 2025-26. The line climbs steeply, reflecting both real output growth and decades of inflation. The 2025-26 value of ₹357.1 lakh crore is a First Advance Estimate from MOSPI and will be revised. The earliest value, ₹10,221.6 crore, was a tiny fraction — the economy has multiplied over three thousand times in nominal terms. This is the most direct answer to the page question, but it must be read with the next chart to separate genuine expansion from price rises.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The headline answer: the total rupee size of everything India produces in a year.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Read the line as the total value of final goods and services produced each year, in current rupees. The steep rise is mostly inflation plus real growth.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read this as ‘purchasing power’ — it’s nominal, not adjusted for price changes over time.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep the y-axis labels sparse and show only every fifth year on the x-axis to avoid clutter on a small screen.

chartExplainers.title: Nominal vs real: how much is just prices?
chartExplainers.takeaway: Real GDP, which removes inflation, was ₹201.9 lakh crore in 2025-26, far below the nominal ₹357.1 lakh crore.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart plots two lines: nominal GDP (current prices) and real GDP (valued at constant 2011-12 prices). Both start in 1950-51, when real GDP was ₹5 lakh crore and nominal was ₹10,221.6 crore. Over the years, the gap widens dramatically. In 2025-26, nominal GDP is ₹357.1 lakh crore, while real GDP is ₹201.9 lakh crore. The difference — about ₹155 lakh crore — is the accumulation of inflation over 75 years. When economists refer to ‘GDP growth’ of around 7%, they mean the change in the real line, not the nominal one. This chart is essential to prevent readers from mistaking price rises for genuine expansion.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The single most misread thing about GDP: most of the headline number’s growth is inflation.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the two lines. The gap between them is inflation. The real line (often shown in a distinct color) shows output growth; the nominal line includes price rises.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not use the nominal line to judge real output expansion; always cite the real line for growth.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use clearly contrasting colors and add a brief annotation explaining the gap at the latest year.

chartExplainers.title: GDP per person
chartExplainers.takeaway: The average Indian’s share of GDP is about ₹2.5 lakh a year, up from ₹404 in the early 1960s.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart divides the total nominal GDP by the estimated population each year, giving per capita GDP in rupees. The earliest point, 1961-62, was ₹404; by 2025-26 it reached ₹2.5 lakh, a display value of roughly ₹2.5 lakh (the locked number is 2,46,144). The line shows a gradual rise, accelerating in the post-2000 period. But this is an arithmetic average, not a real income. It does not mean the typical Indian earns this amount; high earners pull the average far above the median. The chart is vital because it immediately inverts the ‘big economy’ narrative: the same country that is a top-5 total economy becomes low-income per person.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Divide the economy by 1.4 billion people and the picture changes completely.
chartExplainers.howToRead: This is total GDP divided by estimated population. It’s an arithmetic average, not an actual income anyone earns.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not treat it as a ‘salary’ — most people earn far less because of inequality.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a clean line with a clear annotation at the latest point: ‘₹2.5 lakh per person’.

chartExplainers.title: How big, compared with the world
chartExplainers.takeaway: At market exchange rates India is about $3.9 trillion; at purchasing-power parity it is about $17.3 trillion.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart tracks India’s GDP from 1980 to 2025 in two different units: one line shows dollars at the market exchange rate (reaching $3.92 trillion), and the other shows international dollars at purchasing-power parity (reaching $17.3 trillion). Both lines rise, but the PPP line is much higher and grows faster because India’s price level is lower than the US benchmark. Market rates reflect what the economy is worth in global financial markets; PPP reflects the volume of goods and services produced, adjusting for local purchasing power. The ranking flip — 5th vs 3rd — depends entirely on which line is used.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Whether India is the 5th or the 3rd largest economy depends entirely on which ruler you use.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines: one at market exchange rates (blue) and one at PPP (pink). Both correct, different questions.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not mix up the two: PPP is for comparing real living standards, market rates for financial size.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Label the lines with clear text directly on the chart, not just in a legend, for phone readability.

chartExplainers.title: Where India sits among the big economies
chartExplainers.takeaway: India ($3.92 trillion) is now neck-and-neck with the UK ($4.00 trillion), behind Japan ($4.44 trillion) and Germany ($5.05 trillion).
chartExplainers.detail: This multi-line chart plots GDP at market exchange rates for India, China, Germany, Japan, and the UK from 1980 to 2025. India’s line starts low but rises steadily, overtaking the UK around 2022. In 2025, the values are: India $3.92 trillion, UK $4.00 trillion, Japan $4.44 trillion, Germany $5.05 trillion, and China $19.63 trillion. The convergence between India and the UK is visible, but because these are dollar values, exchange rate movements cause the two lines to cross back and forth. The yawning gap to China’s line shows how far India still has to go to become the second-largest economy.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Size made concrete: the economies India has caught and is jostling with.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Multiple lines for different countries at market exchange rates. Watch the convergence and crossing points, e.g., India overtaking the UK.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read this as a fixed ranking — exchange rates cause swap from year to year.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Show only India, UK, Japan and Germany in the main view; perhaps a separate small multiples for China to avoid scale issues.

chartExplainers.title: Big economy, small incomes
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s per capita income ($2,675) is barely above Bangladesh’s ($2,636) and far behind Asian peers.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart plots GDP per capita at market exchange rates for India and five Asian peers from 1980 to 2025. In 2025, India’s $2,675 is the second-lowest in the group, just ahead of Bangladesh’s $2,636. Indonesia ($5,082) and Vietnam ($4,829) are roughly twice as high, China ($13,968) is five times, and South Korea ($36,227) is over twelve times higher. The lines for South Korea and China pulled away sharply after the 1990s, while India’s line rose slowly. This visual makes concrete the core ‘big but poor’ story: India’s total output is large only because its population is enormous.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The other half of the story: enormous in total, near the bottom per person.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Per capita lines for Asian peers. India is near the bottom, despite being a top-5 total economy.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not conclude that Indians are the poorest — this is an average within the formal economy only.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Limit to six lines maximum; annotate the latest value for each briefly.

chartExplainers.title: What India makes: from farms to services
chartExplainers.takeaway: Agriculture fell from 53% to 17% of GVA, while services rose from 31% to 56%.
chartExplainers.detail: Three lines show the share of gross value added from agriculture, industry, and services from 1950-51 to 2025-26. Agriculture started at 53.2% and steadily declined to 16.8%. Services rose from 30.6% to 56.4%, crossing above agriculture in the late 1970s and dominating thereafter. Industry edged up from 16.2% to 26.8%, but never surged. The chart captures India’s deep structural transformation: from a farm economy to a services-led one, without a sustained industrial spike.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The deepest change in the Indian economy since independence: what it produces.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Three lines as shares of GVA. Agriculture fell, services climbed, industry stayed flat.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse GVA with GDP; this is the production view before taxes and subsidies.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use distinct colors and add a brief annotation at the start and end points.

chartExplainers.title: The factories that never came
chartExplainers.takeaway: Manufacturing’s share of GVA has barely moved: 12% in 1951, 14.1% in 2025-26.
chartExplainers.detail: This single line charts manufacturing’s share of gross value added over 75 years. It starts at 11.96% (effectively 12%) and drifts to 14.08% (about 14.1%) today. The line is almost horizontal, in sharp contrast to the East Asian experience, where manufacturing shares soared. India’s path — farms directly to services — skipped the stage that historically creates millions of formal, productive jobs. This is the chart behind the ‘Make in India’ ambition: the segment of the economy that can rapidly absorb low-skilled workers into factories never expanded its footprint.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: India’s most important economic gap: it grew rich in services while skipping the manufacturing stage East Asia built on.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A single flat line: manufacturing share barely moved in 75 years.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume no manufacturing — it grew in absolute terms, but its share of a fast-growing economy stagnated.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Emphasize the flatness with a fixed y-axis from 0 to 25% so the lack of movement is obvious.

chartExplainers.title: Who spends: consumption, investment, trade
chartExplainers.takeaway: Private consumption makes up 61.5% of GDP, investment 30%, government 9.9%, and net exports -2.3%.
chartExplainers.detail: Four lines display the share of GDP going to private consumption, gross fixed capital formation, government consumption, and net exports from 1950-51 to 2025-26. Consumption started extremely high (89%) and has fallen to around 61.5%, while investment rose from 11.4% to 30%. Government spending sits around 10%, and net exports hover near zero — now at -2.3%. The big story is the shift from a near-total consumption economy to one where a larger slice is invested. The investment line is the one that signals future capacity.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The same GDP, seen as who spends the money rather than who produces it.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Four lines as % of GDP. Consumption dominates; investment rose; net exports near zero.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read the net exports line as the trade deficit; it’s the net contribution to GDP, not the balance of trade.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a stacked area or multi-line; clearly mark the latest percentages.

chartExplainers.title: How India opened up to the world
chartExplainers.takeaway: Exports rose from 7% to 21.5% of GDP and imports from 7% to 23.7% after 1991.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines show exports and imports as a share of GDP. Both started near 7% in 1950-51 and stayed flat for decades, reflecting the import-substitution era. After 1991, they break upward: exports reach 21.5% and imports 23.7% by 2025-26. The surge is almost vertical in the early post-reform years. Even though net exports are negative, the chart reveals that behind that small net figure, gross flows have tripled. India now sends out software, pharma, and textiles, and buys in oil, electronics, and machinery — a vastly more open economy.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Behind the small net-exports number are two big, fast-growing flows, and the story of 1991.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Exports and imports as % of GDP. Both tripled after liberalisation.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume openness equals trade surplus; imports almost always exceed exports.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A dual-axis or two-line chart with a shaded vertical line at 1991 to mark the policy change.

chartExplainers.title: Produced here, owned by whom, kept by whom
chartExplainers.takeaway: GNI (₹351.6 lakh crore) is slightly below GDP, but GNDI (₹362 lakh crore) rises above GDP due to remittances.
chartExplainers.detail: Three lines plot GDP, GNI, and GNDI in nominal rupees. GDP is the topmost line in the early years, but over time GNI falls slightly below it (at ₹351.6 lakh crore vs GDP’s ₹357.1 lakh crore in 2025-26). The gap is net primary income paid abroad — profits and interest leaving India. Then GNDI, which adds remittances received from abroad, jumps above both lines to ₹362 lakh crore. The chart uncovers a distinctly Indian feature: workers abroad send back enough money to lift the country’s disposable income above its domestic output.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Three subtly different ways to size the economy, and the gap between them is a very Indian story.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Three lines: GDP > GNI (net outflow of income) > GNDI (remittances push it above GDP).
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse GDP with GNI or GNDI; each answers a different question about where income ends up.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a single chart with three labeled lines; narrow the y-axis range to minimize dead space.

chartExplainers.title: Who saves the money India invests
chartExplainers.takeaway: Households save 18.1% of GDP, private corporates 10.7%, and the public sector only 2%.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the breakdown of gross saving as a percentage of GDP: households, private corporates, and the public sector. The latest data is for 2023-24, where the total saving rate was 30.7% of GDP. Households alone contributed 18.1%, private corporates 10.7%, and the public sector a thin 2%. The household share has declined from 23.6% in 2011-12, but households remain the dominant source of savings — through bank deposits, gold, provident funds, and insurance. This is the supply side of investment: without this household thrift, the 30% investment rate could not be financed domestically.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: India saves about 30% of GDP — among the highest rates anywhere — and that saving is what funds investment.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Stacked or multi-line: households are the big saver, private corporates next, government a small slice.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not think a high saving rate automatically means adequate investment, but it’s a necessary condition.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A stacked bar for the latest year with small time-series sparklines is effective on phones.

chartExplainers.title: How fast it grows, year to year
chartExplainers.takeaway: Real GDP growth in 2025-26 is 7.4%, within the typical 5-8% range but historically volatile.
chartExplainers.detail: A single line chart plots real GDP growth rates year-on-year, from 2012-13 to 2025-26. The earliest reading was 5.5%, and the latest is 7.4%. The line bounces between roughly 5% and 8%, with a steep dip in the pandemic year (a contraction, though not quantified here) and a sharp recovery thereafter. The volatility highlights that India’s growth, while high on average, is not steady — monsoons, global shocks, and policy changes can cause large swings. The 7.4% estimate for 2025-26 is the latest government assessment.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The size is one thing; the speed is what makes headlines.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A single line of real GDP growth rates. Look for the pandemic dip and the rebound.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not extrapolate one year’s growth into the future — it is inherently volatile.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A large, clean line with key annotations for the latest point and the pandemic year.

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: India's economy is simultaneously enormous and poor: add up everything produced and you get the world's ~5th largest economy (₹357 lakh crore), but divide it across 1.4 billion people and the average is only about ₹2.5 lakh a year.
requiredConcepts: India's nominal GDP (output valued at current prices) is about ₹357 lakh crore in 2025-26, up from about ₹10,000 crore in 1950-51; the 2025-26 figure is a First Advance Estimate and will be revised

Real GDP, which strips out inflation by valuing output at constant 2011-12 prices, is about ₹202 lakh crore; the large and widening gap between nominal (₹357 lakh crore) and real (₹202 lakh crore) is accumulated inflation, and when people say the economy 'grew about 7%' they mean the real figure, not the nominal one

By the market exchange rate India's GDP is about $3.9 trillion in 2025 (IMF World Economic Outlook), which makes it around the fifth-largest economy in the world; by purchasing-power parity it is about $17 trillion, the third-largest behind only China and the United States. PPP is larger because the same rupee buys much more in India than a dollar buys in the US, so it adjusts for India's lower price level. Both are correct; they answer different questions

Set against the other big economies (IMF World Economic Outlook, market exchange rate, 2025): India at about $3.9 trillion is around the fifth-largest in the world, essentially neck and neck with the United Kingdom (about $4.0 trillion) and behind Japan (about $4.4 trillion) and Germany (about $5.0 trillion); only the United States (about $31 trillion) and China (about $20 trillion) are far ahead. India overtook the UK around 2022, but because these are market-rate dollars the two trade places from year to year as the rupee and pound move

On GDP per capita the ranking inverts, and this is the core of the 'big but poor' story: India was about $2,675 per person in 2025 (IMF), barely above Bangladesh (about $2,640), with even Indonesia and Vietnam (about $4,800 to $5,100) already ahead, China roughly five times higher (about $14,000) and South Korea more than ten times higher (about $36,000). India is large as a country and poor as a people because a large output is divided among a very large population

Reconcile the two per-capita figures in one passing clause so a reader who converts is not confused: the about ₹2.5 lakh per person (MOSPI, current rupees, 2025-26) and the about $2,700 per person (IMF, current US dollars, 2025) are the same income expressed in two currencies; they do not divide to exactly the market exchange rate only because the two come from slightly different sources, base years and population estimates. Do not labour this point

GDP per person is about ₹2.5 lakh a year in 2025-26, up from a few hundred rupees in the early 1960s; this is an arithmetic average (GDP divided by population), NOT a salary or a typical income, and it sits well above what most Indians actually earn because a small number of high earners pull the average up. Inequality is covered in depth on a separate page, so here just flag honestly that the average hides it

The production structure has transformed since independence: agriculture's share of gross value added fell from about 53% in 1951 to about 17% now; services rose from about 31% to about 56%; industry is about 27%. Services is now the dominant sector

Manufacturing is the key structural gap: its share of output has barely moved in 75 years, from about 12% in 1951 to about 14% now. Unlike China, South Korea or Japan, India never became a factory-led economy; it shifted from farms largely to services, skipping the mass-manufacturing stage. This is the backdrop to 'Make in India'

A crucial mismatch: agriculture is only about 17% of output but still supports a far larger share of India's workers, so output shifted to services and industry while most people stayed in low-productivity farm work (the jobs question is a separate page; flag the mismatch, do not over-claim it here)

GDP can be read from the spending side as private consumption + investment + government spending + net exports. For India in 2025-26 these are roughly: private final consumption about 61% of GDP, gross fixed capital formation (investment) about 30%, government consumption about 10%, and net exports about minus 2% because imports (about 24% of GDP) slightly exceed exports (about 21%). India is a consumption-led economy, and the investment share is what economists watch most as the seed of future growth

GVA versus GDP: gross value added is the producers' view (what each sector adds), and GDP equals total GVA plus taxes on products minus subsidies on products. That is why the sector chart is labelled GVA while the headline is GDP

GDP measures output produced inside India's borders; Gross National Income (GNI) measures income accruing to Indians. India's GNI is about ₹352 lakh crore, roughly ₹5.5 lakh crore below GDP, because India pays out more primary income (profits, interest, dividends) to the rest of the world than it receives. The gap is small for India, but for economies dominated by foreign-owned firms or large remittance-sending diasporas GDP and GNI can diverge a lot

Gross National Disposable Income (GNDI) extends the income picture one step further and reveals a distinctly Indian twist: while GNI (about ₹352 lakh crore) sits just below GDP because of income paid to foreigners, GNDI rises BACK ABOVE GDP to about ₹362 lakh crore, because remittances sent home by Indians working abroad more than offset that outflow. India's disposable income is therefore larger than its domestic output, a hallmark of a major remittance-receiving economy

India's openness to trade was transformed after the 1991 liberalisation: exports of goods and services rose from about 7% of GDP through the closed pre-reform decades to about 21% today, and imports to about 24%; both flows roughly tripled as a share of the economy even though net exports remain slightly negative, because a more open India both sells far more to and buys far more from the world

India saves about 30% of GDP, among the highest rates in the world, and domestic saving is what finances domestic investment. Households (not companies or the government) do most of it: households are about 59% of gross saving, private corporations about 35%, and the public sector only about 6%. Ordinary households finance India's growth through bank deposits, gold, provident funds, insurance and property

Real GDP growth is usually in the 5 to 8% range and is volatile: it fell about 5.8% in 2020-21 during the COVID lockdown (a genuine contraction, not just slower growth) and rebounded strongly afterwards; India is among the fastest-growing large economies but a single shock such as a drought or a global crisis can swing the annual figure hard

What GDP does NOT tell you, stated plainly at the close: it does not show who gets the income (distribution and inequality), it misses most unpaid work such as housework and care, it does not net out environmental damage or resource depletion, it says nothing about the quality or informality of jobs (a large part of India's economy is informal and imperfectly measured), and it ignores regional gaps between states. GDP is an excellent measure of the SIZE and GROWTH of an economy and a poor measure of its wellbeing or fairness

Honesty caveats to weave in, not dump: the latest-year national-accounts figures are advance estimates revised several times before they are final; the real series is on the 2011-12 base; per-capita is GDP divided by World Bank mid-year population; and the PPP figure is a modelled price-level adjustment, not a market value

sectionPlan.heading: How big is India's economy in rupees?
sectionPlan.job: Show the total rupee size of everything India produces in a year, from 1950-51 to today, and establish the headline scale.
conceptsToExplain: Nominal GDP is total output valued at current market prices, unadjusted for inflation.
The Indian economy has grown from about ₹10,000 crore to over ₹357 lakh crore since 1950-51.
sectionPlan.caveat: The 2025-26 figure is a First Advance Estimate and will be revised.

sectionPlan.heading: How much of that number is just prices?
sectionPlan.job: Distinguish nominal GDP from real GDP (inflation-adjusted) to reveal that most of the headline growth is due to inflation rather than an actual increase in output.
conceptsToExplain: Real GDP values output at constant prices (2011-12 base) to strip out inflation.
The large gap between nominal and real GDP is accumulated inflation.
When economists say the economy 'grew 7%', they mean real GDP growth, not nominal.
sectionPlan.caveat: The real series uses a fixed base year (2011-12) and the choice can affect the measured gap.

sectionPlan.heading: What does that mean per person?
sectionPlan.job: Divide the economy by the population to get GDP per capita, an arithmetic average that hides inequality but is the starting point for measuring individual prosperity.
conceptsToExplain: GDP per capita is total GDP divided by mid-year population; it is NOT a typical income.
India's per capita income has risen from a few hundred rupees to about ₹2.5 lakh, but the average is pulled up by high earners.
sectionPlan.caveat: This average says nothing about income distribution; inequality is substantial.

sectionPlan.heading: How does India compare globally?
sectionPlan.job: Compare India's GDP at market exchange rates and at purchasing-power parity to explain why the ranking changes from 5th to 3rd largest.
lockedNumbersToUse: India GDP at market exchange rate, latest
India GDP at purchasing-power parity, latest
conceptsToExplain: Market exchange rates convert rupees at the going forex rate, while PPP adjusts for differing purchasing power.
PPP is larger because goods and services are generally cheaper in India than in the US.
sectionPlan.caveat: PPP is a modelled adjustment, not a market value; both measures are valid for different purposes.

sectionPlan.heading: Where does India stand among the big economies?
sectionPlan.job: Set India side-by-side with China, Germany, Japan, the UK, etc., to show its relative size and the narrowing gaps.
lockedNumbersToUse: United Kingdom — GDP (market exchange rate), latest
conceptsToExplain: At market exchange rates, India (~$3.9 trillion) is roughly alongside the UK (~$4 trillion) and behind Japan (~$4.4 trillion).
sectionPlan.caveat: These dollar values swing with exchange rates, so rankings can change year to year.

sectionPlan.heading: Why is India still poor per person?
sectionPlan.job: Show per capita GDP across Asian peers to illustrate that despite being a large economy, India's average income is near the bottom among its peers.
lockedNumbersToUse: India — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
China — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
South Korea — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
Bangladesh — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
Indonesia — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
Vietnam — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
conceptsToExplain: A large output divided by a very large population yields a small per capita figure.
India at $2,675 per person is barely above Bangladesh ($2,640) and far behind East Asian economies.
sectionPlan.caveat: Per capita GDP is an average; cross-country comparisons don't capture income distribution or purchasing power differences.

sectionPlan.heading: What does India actually produce?
sectionPlan.job: Trace the structural transformation from an agrarian to a services-dominated economy, using shares of gross value added.
conceptsToExplain: Gross Value Added (GVA) measures the contribution of each sector before taxes and subsidies.
Agriculture's share fell from 53% to 17%, while services rose from 31% to 56%.
The sector chart uses GVA, not GDP; GDP = GVA + net product taxes.
sectionPlan.caveat: Sector shares are in nominal terms; real structural shifts may look slightly different.

sectionPlan.heading: Why didn't India become a factory nation?
sectionPlan.job: Spotlight manufacturing's stagnant share of GVA (barely moved from 12% to 14%) to highlight India's skipped industrialisation stage.
conceptsToExplain: Unlike East Asian economies, India moved directly from farms to services, bypassing mass manufacturing.
Manufacturing has never accounted for more than a small slice of the economy, limiting formal job creation.
sectionPlan.caveat: Manufacturing includes only formal sector production; informal manufacturing may be undercounted.

sectionPlan.heading: Who is doing the spending?
sectionPlan.job: Break down GDP by expenditure: private consumption, investment, government, and net exports, to show that India is a consumption-led economy.
lockedNumbersToUse: Private consumption as % of GDP, latest
Gross fixed capital formation as % of GDP, latest
Government consumption as % of GDP, latest
Net exports as % of GDP, latest
conceptsToExplain: GDP measured from the spending side = C + I + G + (X-M).
India's private consumption is about 61% of GDP, investment about 30%, government 10%, net exports -2%.
Investment (fixed capital formation) is watched as the seed of future growth.
sectionPlan.caveat: The expenditure approach should sum to GDP, but statistical discrepancies exist.

sectionPlan.heading: How did trade reshape the economy?
sectionPlan.job: Show how exports and imports as a share of GDP surged after 1991, tripling from about 7% to over 21% and 24% respectively.
conceptsToExplain: Although net exports are small and negative, gross trade flows have risen dramatically.
Liberalisation opened India to global markets, transforming its external sector.
sectionPlan.caveat: Trade shares can be sensitive to exchange rate swings and global commodity prices.

sectionPlan.heading: Does the output belong to Indians?
sectionPlan.job: Clarify the difference between GDP, GNI, and GNDI: output produced in India vs income accruing to Indians vs income after remittances.
lockedNumbersToUse: Gross National Disposable Income, nominal, latest
conceptsToExplain: GDP is domestic production; GNI adjusts for income paid abroad and received from abroad.
India's GNI (₹351.6 lakh crore) is about ₹5.5 lakh crore less than GDP, but GNDI (₹362 lakh crore) is higher due to remittances.
sectionPlan.caveat: The gap between GDP and GNI is small for India, but remittances make GNDI the broadest income measure.

sectionPlan.heading: Who saves the money that India invests?
sectionPlan.job: Show that India's high saving rate (~30% of GDP) is primarily driven by households, not companies or the government.
lockedNumbersToUse: Household saving as % of GDP, latest
Private-corporate saving as % of GDP, latest
Public-sector saving as % of GDP, latest
Gross saving rate (% of GDP), latest
conceptsToExplain: Gross saving finances gross capital formation; India's saving rate is among the world's highest.
Households contribute the largest share (about 18% of GDP), then private corporates (11%), and public sector (2%).
sectionPlan.caveat: Saving data often lags GDP data; latest figures are for 2023-24.

sectionPlan.heading: How fast is India's economy growing?
sectionPlan.job: Present real GDP growth rates year-on-year, highlighting its typical range (5-8%) and volatility, including the 2020-21 contraction.
conceptsToExplain: Real GDP growth measures how much output increased after removing inflation.
Growth is volatile: it fell to -5.8% in 2020-21 during the pandemic, then rebounded sharply.
sectionPlan.caveat: Growth rates are annual YoY; quarterly patterns can be lumpy.

sectionPlan.heading: What does GDP still not tell us?
sectionPlan.job: Honestly list what GDP misses: inequality, unpaid work, environmental damage, informal jobs, regional disparities, and wellbeing.
conceptsToExplain: GDP is a production measure, not a welfare measure.
It excludes distribution, non-market work, environmental costs, and quality of life.
sectionPlan.caveat: This section is not derived from any chart; it ties the whole page together.

chartPlan.title: India's economy, 1950-51 to today
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The headline answer: the total rupee size of everything India produces in a year.
chartPlan.howToRead: Read the line as the total value of final goods and services produced each year, in current rupees. The steep rise is mostly inflation plus real growth.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read this as 'purchasing power'—it's nominal, not adjusted for price changes over time.

chartPlan.title: Nominal vs real: how much is just prices?
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The single most misread thing about GDP: most of the headline number's growth is inflation.
chartPlan.howToRead: Compare the two lines. The gap between them is inflation. The real line (pink) shows output growth; the nominal (blue) includes price rises.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not use the nominal line to judge real output expansion; always cite the real line for growth.

chartPlan.title: GDP per person
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Divide the economy by 1.4 billion people and the picture changes completely.
chartPlan.howToRead: This is total GDP divided by estimated population. It's an arithmetic average, not an actual income anyone earns.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not treat it as a 'salary'—most people earn far less because of inequality.

chartPlan.title: How big, compared with the world
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Whether India is the 5th or the 3rd largest economy depends entirely on which ruler you use.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: one at market exchange rates (blue) and one at PPP (pink). Both correct, different questions.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not mix up the two: PPP is for comparing real living standards, market rates for financial size.

chartPlan.title: Where India sits among the big economies
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Size made concrete: the economies India has caught and is jostling with.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multiple lines for different countries at market exchange rates. Watch the convergence and crossing points, e.g., India overtaking the UK.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read this as a fixed ranking—exchange rates cause swap from year to year.

chartPlan.title: Big economy, small incomes
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The other half of the story: enormous in total, near the bottom per person.
chartPlan.howToRead: Per capita lines for Asian peers. India is near the bottom, despite being a top-5 total economy.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not conclude that Indians are the poorest—here is an average within the formal economy only.

chartPlan.title: What India makes: from farms to services
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The deepest change in the Indian economy since independence: what it produces.
chartPlan.howToRead: Three lines as shares of GVA. Agriculture fell, services climbed, industry stayed flat.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse GVA with GDP; this is the production view before taxes and subsidies.

chartPlan.title: The factories that never came
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India's most important economic gap: it grew rich in services while skipping the manufacturing stage East Asia built on.
chartPlan.howToRead: A single flat line: manufacturing share barely moved in 75 years.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume no manufacturing—it grew in absolute terms, but its share of a fast-growing economy stagnated.

chartPlan.title: Who spends: consumption, investment, trade
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The same GDP, seen as who spends the money rather than who produces it.
chartPlan.howToRead: Four lines as % of GDP. Consumption dominates; investment rose; net exports near zero.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read the net exports line as the trade deficit; it's the net contribution to GDP, not the balance of trade.

chartPlan.title: How India opened up to the world
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Behind the small net-exports number are two big, fast-growing flows, and the story of 1991.
chartPlan.howToRead: Exports and imports as % of GDP. Both tripled after liberalisation.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume openness equals trade surplus; imports almost always exceed exports.

chartPlan.title: Produced here, owned by whom, kept by whom
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Three subtly different ways to size the economy, and the gap between them is a very Indian story.
chartPlan.howToRead: Three lines: GDP > GNI (net outflow of income) > GNDI (remittances push it above GDP).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse GDP with GNI or GNDI; each answers a different question about where income ends up.

chartPlan.title: Who saves the money India invests
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India saves about 30% of GDP — among the highest rates anywhere — and that saving is what funds investment.
chartPlan.howToRead: Stacked or multi-line: households are the big saver, private corporates next, government a small slice.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not think a high saving rate automatically means adequate investment, but it's a necessary condition.

chartPlan.title: How fast it grows, year to year
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The size is one thing; the speed is what makes headlines.
chartPlan.howToRead: A single line of real GDP growth rates. Look for the pandemic dip and the rebound.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not extrapolate one year's growth into the future—it is inherently volatile.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Long historical trends need annual data to show broad sweep; quarterly would overcrowd a phone screen.
The widening gap is visible only over decades.
Per capita trends are slow; annual is sufficient.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: How big compared with the world
visualizationDecisions.reason: PPP vs market rates need to be compared over a long period.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: Where India sits among the big economies
visualizationDecisions.reason: Cross-country ranking changes need a multi-decade view.
Per capita comparisons need long windows to see divergence.
Structural transformation is a slow process; annual shares show the trend clearly.
Stagnation of manufacturing share is a long-run story.
Expenditure shares change gradually; annual data avoids quarterly noise.
The post-1991 surge is clear with annual data; quarterly would obscure the break.
Gaps between GDP, GNI, GNDI are best seen over time.
Saving shares are available annually and move slowly.
Volatility needs annual data; quarterly growth rates are too noisy for a general page.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given period. Think of it as the size of the cake baked by all factories, farms, and offices inside India. It includes only final products, not intermediate goods used up in production.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: This is the central measure of the page; everything flows from understanding what GDP counts and what it misses.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: GDP divided by the population. It's like slicing that cake equally among every Indian, but the slices are not actually equal. It's an average, not a typical income.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Used to compare prosperity across countries and to show the 'big but poor' tension.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Nominal GDP values output at today's prices. Real GDP uses prices from a base year (2011-12) to remove inflation, so it measures actual production growth. If a movie ticket costs more this year but you watch the same number of movies, nominal spending rises but real consumption stays flat.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Explains why the headline GDP number is so huge: most of it is price hikes, not extra output.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: An adjustment that compares how much money can actually buy in different countries. A haircut costs much less in India than in the US, so PPP shows a truer comparison of living standards by valuing both economies at the same price level.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Shifts India's global ranking from around 5th (market rates) to 3rd (PPP), and makes cross-country income comparisons more meaningful.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The value that producers (sectors like agriculture, manufacturing, services) add to raw materials and inputs. GVA is GDP minus net taxes on products. It's the production-side view, while GDP adds the tax component.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: All sector share charts are in GVA, so understanding the difference prevents confusion with the headline GDP number.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Total income earned by a country's residents, whether from domestic production or from overseas (e.g., profits of Indian companies abroad minus similar outflows). GDP is 'produced in India'; GNI is 'earned by Indians'. The difference is net primary income from abroad.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Shows that a small chunk of India's domestic output actually goes to foreigners as profits or interest.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number

plannedCharts.title: India's economy, 1950-51 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline answer: the total rupee size of everything India produces in a year.

plannedCharts.title: Nominal vs real: how much is just prices?
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · GDP at current prices vs at constant 2011-12 prices
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single most misread thing about GDP: most of the headline number's growth is inflation.

plannedCharts.title: GDP per person
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Divide the economy by 1.4 billion people and the picture changes completely.

plannedCharts.title: How big, compared with the world
plannedCharts.subtitle: IMF World Economic Outlook · India's GDP at market exchange rates vs at purchasing-power parity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether India is the 5th or the 3rd largest economy depends entirely on which ruler you use.

plannedCharts.title: Where India sits among the big economies
plannedCharts.subtitle: IMF World Economic Outlook · GDP at market exchange rates, 1980 to 2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Size made concrete: the economies India has caught and is jostling with.

plannedCharts.title: Big economy, small incomes
plannedCharts.subtitle: IMF World Economic Outlook · GDP per capita at market exchange rates, India vs Asian peers
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The other half of the story: enormous in total, near the bottom per person.

plannedCharts.title: What India makes: from farms to services
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · share of gross value added by sector
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The deepest change in the Indian economy since independence: what it produces.

plannedCharts.title: The factories that never came
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's most important economic gap: it grew rich in services while skipping the manufacturing stage East Asia built on.

plannedCharts.title: Who spends: consumption, investment, trade
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · expenditure components as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The same GDP, seen as who spends the money rather than who produces it.

plannedCharts.title: How India opened up to the world
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · exports and imports of goods & services as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Behind the small net-exports number are two big, fast-growing flows, and the story of 1991.

plannedCharts.title: Produced here, owned by whom, kept by whom
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · GDP vs GNI vs GNDI
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Three subtly different ways to size the economy, and the gap between them is a very Indian story.

plannedCharts.title: Who saves the money India invests
plannedCharts.subtitle: MOSPI · gross saving by who does the saving
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India saves about 30% of GDP — among the highest rates anywhere — and that saving is what funds investment.

plannedCharts.title: How fast it grows, year to year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The size is one thing; the speed is what makes headlines.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
